coredata.dc:title: N-doped carbon dots/H2O2 chemiluminescence system for selective detection of Fe2+ ion in environmental samples
coredata.dc:creator: Shah, Syed Niaz Ali
Dou, Xiangnan
Khan, Mashooq
Uchiyama, Katsumi
Lin, Jin-Ming
coredata.dc:description: The nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) produces strong chemiluminescence (CL)-emission due to hydroxyl radical (•OH) induced electron-hole transition in N-CDs. The Fe2+ has the ability to generate •OH from available hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Therefore, a pre-mixed N-CDs/H2O2 solution was utilized for selective quantification of Fe2+ in solution via CL-emission. A linear increase in the CL-emission intensity was observed within increase in Fe2+ concentration. The N-CDs/H2O2 system enabled the detection of Fe2+ up to lower concentration of 0.2 × 10−9 M with a linear dynamic range of 1.0 × 10−9–1.0 × 10−6 M. Significantly, no CL-emission was observed when other divalent cations, Al3+, Fe3+, or Cr3+ were injected to this system. Moreover, no interference was observed when a mixed solution of Fe2+ and other cations were introduced to N-CDs/H2O2. The practical evaluation of N-CDs/H2O2 system was demonstrated for detection of Fe2+ in tap, lotus pond, and canal water samples. The easy detection, high sensitivity, and selectivity make this method a significant tool for analysis of Fe2+ in solution.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Chemiluminescence
Carbon dots
Fe2+ detection
Hydroxyl radical

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Selectivity toward Fe2+ and real sample analysis

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Effect of Fe2+ ion on CL intensity of H2O2 in the presence of N-CDs (i) Fe2+ injected to N-CDs (ii) Fe2+ injected to H2O2 (iii) Fe2+ injected to N-CDs-H2O2 (iv) Fe3+ injected to N-CDs-H2O2. The concentration of Fe2+ and Fe3+ was 0.01 M while H2O2 was 0.1 M. The volume of each reagent used was 100 µL. (b) Enhancement of CL intensity from Fenton system by N-CDs. (c) PL spectra of N-CDs. Excitation and emission spectra of N-CDs. (d) CL spectra of N-CDs enhanced Fenton system. The concentration of Fe2+ was 0.1 M and H2O2 was 0.1 M. The flow rate was 3 mL/min for each reagent.

EPR analysis of the N-CDs enhanced CL system. (a) DMPO/•OH adduct in (i) Fe2++H2O2 (ii) Fe2++H2O2+N-CDs. (b) TMP/1O2 adduct. (i) Fe2++H2O2 (ii) Fe2++H2O2+N-CDs. The concentration of Fe2+ was 0.001 M, H2O2 and DMPO was 0.1 M, while TMP was 10% by volume in water. The volume of each reagent was 50 µL.

Analytical figure of merits of N-CDs enhanced Fenton system (a) Effect of different concentration of Fe2+ on CL intensity of Fe2+-H2O2-N-CDs system. The concentration of Fe2+ was (i) 10−2 M (ii) 10−3 M (iii) 10−4 M (iv) 10−5 M (v) 10−6 M (vi) 10−7 M (vii) 10−8 M (viii) 10−9 M and (ix) water. (b) Calibration curve of Fe2+ ion. There was linear relation between CL intensity and concentration of Fe2+ in range of 10−6–10−9 M. The concentration of H2O2 was 0.1 M. The volume of each reagent used was 100 µL.

Selectivity analyses for Fe2+ ion over other metal ions (a) CL intensity of N-CDs-H2O2 aqueous solution in presence of different metal ions (metal ions were 10−3 M). (b) Interference ion study of N-CDs enhanced Fenton system. Bar graph of the CL intensity of N-CDs mixed H2O2 with respective interfering cations in the presence of 10−3 M Fe2+ ion. (c) Bar graph of the CL intensity of N-CDs mixed H2O2 with respective interfering anions in the presence of 10−3 M Fe2+ ion. The concentration of metal ions and anions were 10−3 M while H2O2 was 0.1 M. The volume of each reagent was 100 µL. The results are average of triplicate readings.
CL intensity of tap, lotus pond, canal and deionized water samples.
Recovery of Fe2+ ions in real water samples.

ce:legend.ce:simple-para: The recovery results of Fe2+ ion in the above mentioned samples (W1-W3) were average of triplicate readings.

head.ce:title: N-doped carbon dots/H2O2 chemiluminescence system for selective detection of Fe2+ ion in environmental samples

ce:affiliation.ce:label: a
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, MOE Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University, Beijing 100084, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Beijing Key Laboratory of Microanalytical Methods and Instrumentation, MOE Key Laboratory of Bioorganic Phosphorus Chemistry & Chemical Biology, Department of Chemistry, Tsinghua University

ce:affiliation.ce:label: b
ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Urban Environmental Sciences, Tokyo Metropolitan University, Minamiohsawa, Hachioji, Tokyo 192-0397, Japan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Department of Applied Chemistry, Graduate School of Urban Environmental Sciences, Tokyo Metropolitan University

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: The nitrogen doped carbon dots (N-CDs) produces strong chemiluminescence (CL)-emission due to hydroxyl radical (•OH) induced electron-hole transition in N-CDs. The Fe2+ has the ability to generate •OH from available hydrogen peroxide (H2O2). Therefore, a pre-mixed N-CDs/H2O2 solution was utilized for selective quantification of Fe2+ in solution via CL-emission. A linear increase in the CL-emission intensity was observed within increase in Fe2+ concentration. The N-CDs/H2O2 system enabled the detection of Fe2+ up to lower concentration of 0.2 × 10−9 M with a linear dynamic range of 1.0 × 10−9–1.0 × 10−6 M. Significantly, no CL-emission was observed when other divalent cations, Al3+, Fe3+, or Cr3+ were injected to this system. Moreover, no interference was observed when a mixed solution of Fe2+ and other cations were introduced to N-CDs/H2O2. The practical evaluation of N-CDs/H2O2 system was demonstrated for detection of Fe2+ in tap, lotus pond, and canal water samples. The easy detection, high sensitivity, and selectivity make this method a significant tool for analysis of Fe2+ in solution.

The N-CDs enhanced Fenton system was used for the sensitive and selective determination of Fe2+ ion in the presence of various cations and anions.
fx1

ce:section.ce:para: Iron (Fe) is the fourth most abundant element in the earth's crust by weight and is an essential micronutrient in human body that carries oxygen and forms part of the hemoglobin, myoglobin, and enzymes. Natural waters contain variable amounts of Fe depending on the geological area and other chemical components of the waterway. The Fe2+ and Fe3+ ions are the primary forms of concern in the aquatic environment. In groundwater Fe is normally present as a soluble Fe2+ ion. It appears to be more of a nuisance than a potential health hazard and the high Fe2+ contents increases the risk of chronic diseases. According to World Health Organization the thresholds of Fe in drinking water is 1.8 × 10−6 M for Fe2+ and 3.6 × 10−6 M for Fe3+ [1]. Previously, a plethora of methods such as colorimetric [2,3], fluorescent [4], colorimetric [1], chromatographic [5], electrochemical [6], and spectroscopic (Mossbauer [7] and flame atomic absorption spectroscopy [8]) were used for the detection of Fe2+/Fe3+ in solution. Among these the colorimetric 1–10-pehnanthroline and ferrozine assays were frequently utilized due to its simplicity [2,3]. Viollier et al. modified ferrozine method for determination of Fe2+/Fe3+ in small volume of fresh and marine water [9]. Rhodamine and quinolone functionalized nano film was used for quantitative detection of Fe3+ [10]. A hybrid oxidimetric/colorimetric approach was utilized for the detection of total Fe [11]. The quantitative determination of Fe2+ ion is a challenge in analytical chemistry. Several chemiluminescence (CL) based system for selective detection of Fe2+ have been devised. For example, Yamada et al. utilized brilliant sulfoflavine-H2O2-NaOH-acetonitrile flow analysis technique for determination of Fe2+ [12]. Later on, Erold et al. modified this method to enhance sensitivity [13]. However, these methods have the limitations of interference from other divalent cations, co-contaminants, and strong complexing agents [14]. Therefore, the design of expedient and inexpensive approaches for sensitive and selective detection of Fe2+ with rapid and easy manipulation is required.

Carbon dots (CDs) have become a popular class of carbon nanomaterials and has been widely used in bio-imaging, cell-labeling, and sensing because of its non-toxicity, eco-friendliness, high fluorescence, biocompatibility, and fast response times [15,16]. CDs has been used for the detection of glucose, dopamine, glutathione, phytic acid, cholesterol, and metals ions [17]. These developments were based on exploiting the “off–on” fluorescence properties of CDs [18]. Recent reports have explored the fascinating CL properties of CDs [19–23]. Nevertheless, research on CL properties of CDs is to enhance the inherent sensitivity of weak CL emission to widen its applications. Our group established a CL system of high emission intensity, by introducing nitrogen doped-CDs (N-CDs) into Fenton system [24]. This work revealed a new connection between N-CDs and Fenton reaction. It was observed that, the Fe2+ was quickly consumed and slowly regenerated prior to establish equilibrium between Fe2+ and Fe3+. The reversible conversion of Fe2+⇔Fe3+ was dependent and controllable by the amount of reactive oxygen species in the system. We envisioned that, this controllable redox system could be utilized for the selective and quantitative detection of Fe2+.

Herein, the Fe2+ in aqueous medium was quantified from the CL-emission intensity obtained from the addition of Fe2+ solution into a pre-mixed N-CDs and H2O2 (N-CDs/H2O2) solution. The CL intensity increased within increase in Fe2+ concentration. The N-CDs/H2O2 system enabled the detection of Fe2+ without significant interference from Fe3+ and tested cations. The detection of Fe2+ was also performed in real water sample collected from tap, lotus pond, and canal. This method offers an advantage over other techniques for selective and quantitative determinations by eliminating time consuming steps.

Hydrogen peroxide (H2O2), potassium bromide (KBr), and mercuric chloride (HgCl2) (Beijing Chemical Reagent Co. Beijing, China), cobalt sulfate (CoSO4), copper sulfate (CuSO4), and sodium chloride (NaCl) (Beijing Chemical Industry, Beijing, China), ferric chloride (FeCl3), and zinc chloride (ZnCl2) (Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co. Ltd. Shanghai, China), magnesium sulfate (MgSO4) (3rd Chemical Industry, Tianjin, China), cadmium chloride (CdCl2), aluminum nitrate (Al2(NO3)3, and ferrous sulfate (FeSO4) (Heng Ye Jingxi Chemical Co. Ltd. Hebei, China), nickel sulfate (NiSO4) and lead nitrate (Pb(NO3)2) (Beijing Shiji Chemical Industry, Beijing, China), chromium chloride (CrCl3) (Aladdin Industrial Corporation, Shanghai, China), calcium sulfate (CaSO4) (Alfa Aesar, United State), histidine (Dingguochansheng Biotech. Co. Ltd. Beijing, China), 5,5-dimethyl-1-pyrroline-N-oxide (DMPO) (Tokyo Kasei Kogyo, Japan), and 2,2,6,6-tetramethyl-4-piperidine (TMP) (Sigma-Aldrich) were of analytical grade and used as received. Deionized water (DIW) was used for solution preparation in all experiments.

The N-CDs were synthesized as reported in our previous work [21]. Briefly, 0.1 M aqueous solution of histidine was transferred into Teflon lined autoclave and heated at temperature 240 °C for 8 h. The color of the solution was changed from colorless to dark brown. The dialysis was performed by using Biotech regenerated cellulose membrane (MW 3.5 kDa) in DIW for 12 h with continuous stirring to remove the unreacted reagents. The DIW was replaced after each 2 h. The prepared N-CDs suspension was obtained and characterized using transmission electron microscope (TEM, ESI Fig. S1), photoluminescence (PL), and UV–visible spectroscopy (ESI Fig. S2). The average size of the N-CDs was 5 nm. The UV–visible spectrum shows the characteristic absorption bands at 295 and 270 nm due to π-π* and n-π* electron transitions, respectively. The N-CDs showed blue color under UV lamp (Inset Fig. S2).

The CL profiles were obtained by batch experiments and integrated at 0.1 s intervals with 1300 V negative voltage of the photomultiplier tube. 100 µL N-CDs and 100 µL of H2O2 (0.1 M) were premixed in a glass cuvette followed by the injection of 100 µL of Fe2+ (different concentration) through a microliter syringe from the upper injection port by batch method. Stock solution (10−2 M) of Fe2+ was prepared from ferrous sulfate heptahydrate (FeSO4.7H2O) in water. Working standards were prepared by diluting the stock solution with DI water. The analysis of Fe2+ in solution was carried out at different concentration ranging from 1 × 10−9 to 1 × 10−2 M.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Selectivity toward Fe2+ and real sample analysis
ce:section.ce:para: Selectivity analyses were carried out by batch CL analysis. A 1 × 10−3 M solution of each Fe3+, Cr3+, Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, K+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Al3+, Na+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Ni2+ were injected in a premixed N-CDs/H2O2 solution in separate experiments and the CL-emission intensity was analyzed. In order to find the interference from each ion, a 1 × 10−3 M solution of each ion were individually mixed with 1 × 10−3 M Fe2+ solution and injected to N-CDs/H2O2 solution for CL-emission analysis.

For the analysis of Fe2+ in real samples, water samples from three different sources tap (W1), lotus pond (W2) and canal (W3) were collected. Real water samples were collected from Tsinghua Lotus Pond and from Canal (Tsinghua University, Beijing, China). All samples were filtered through a microporous (0.45 mm) membrane to remove the colloidal particles. Then 100 µL of each W1, W2, and W3 were injected in N-CDs/H2O2 solution in separate experiments and the CL-emission intensity was analyzed. For all experiments 100 µL of each N-CDs and H2O2 (0.1 M) was mixed.

The CL kinetic curves were obtained by batch experiments in glass cuvette. The CL profiles were done by BPCL Luminescence Analyzer (Institute of Biophysics, Chinese Academy of Sciences, Beijing, China) and displayed at 1300 V negative voltage of the photomultiplier tube and integrated at 0.1 s intervals. The transmission electron microscopy images were captured using TEM (Tecnai G2 20S-Twin, FEI Company, USA) at 200 kV. The CL spectra were obtained with a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan) to know the effect of CL signals as a function of wavelength. The setup consisted of two peristaltic pumps, PMT detector, and flow cell placed inside the cell holder of the spectrophotometer. The emission slit width was 20 nm and the source lamps of the instrument were turned off during CL-detection. The UV–visible absorption spectra of N-CDs were recorded by UV–visible spectrometer (UV-3900, Hitachi, Japan). The photo-luminescent (PL) analysis was carried out by a fluorescence spectrophotometer (F-7000, Hitachi, Japan). Electron Paramagnetic Resonance (EPR) spectroscopy was carried out (using JEOL JES-FA200 spectrometer. conditions: microwave power, 1.0 mW; modulation amplitude, 1.0 G; and receiver gain, 1.00e + 05) by mixing (50 µL of each) N-CDs, H2O2 (0.1 M) and DMPO (0.1 M) followed by the addition of Fe2+ (0.01 M) or otherwise specified. The sample tube was filled from the reaction mixture and the analysis was carried out at room temperature. For the determination of singlet oxygen, the same procedure was carried out with TMP (10% by volume in water) instead of DMPO. All experiments were performed in triplicate and their standard deviation was calculated.

Fig. 1a shows the CL intensity when 100 µL of Fe2+ (0.01 M) was injected at various conditions. No significant CL intensity was observed when Fe2+ was injected to N-CDs (Fig. 1ai) or H2O2 (Fig. 1aii) solutions. A high CL emission intensity was observed when Fe2+ was added to a premixed N-CDs/H2O2 solution (100 µL of each, H2O2 (0.1 M)) (Fig. 1aiii). The three lines for each analysis indicate the real time emissions for a set of three experiments. The CL signals reached to its maximum intensity in 0.3 s followed by a slow decrease. The abrupt increase was due to the fast reaction between Fe2+ and H2O2 and the equilibrium between Fe2+ and Fe3+ resulted in slow decrease in CL intensity [24]. The high CL intensity by introduction of Fe2+ was due to the generation of hydroxyl radical (•OH) from the reaction of Fe2+ and H2O2 as illustrated in reaction 1. The •OH then oxidizes the N-CDs [25], which facilitate the generation of reactive oxygen species (ROS) from the available H2O2 [26]. The generated ROS induces electron-hole transition in N-CDs lead to a strong CL-emission as schematized in Fig. 1b. The pathway of oxygen incorporation was assumed to proceed via 2-oxo-histidinyl radical intermediate that finally led to 2-oxo-histidine. Indeed, oxidation by H2O2 led to rapid incorporation of oxygen to N-CDs. This mechanism would rely on production of •OH radicals through Fenton reaction [27,28]. Thus, •OH radical was responsible for induction of electron-hole in the conduction and valence band of N-CDs, which is responsible for high CL-emission. Previously, it was reported that singlet oxygen (1O2) produces from the decomposition of H2O2 is responsible for CL-emission in Fenton system [24,29]. In order to confirm that in the present system the strong CL-emission is from the oxidation of N-CDs and not from 1O2, the PL and CL-emission spectra of the N-CDs coupled Fenton system were recorded. The PL-emission at wavelength (λ) = 466 nm was observed at excitation λ = 366 nm (Fig. 1c). Similarly, the CL-spectra show a strong emission at λ = 466 nm for N-CDs coupled Fenton system (Fig. 1d). The emission in both PL and CL spectra at same λ confirmed that the strong CL-emission was from N-CDs. The N-CDs is the origin of CL-emission in this system was further verified using EPR spectroscopy. The EPR analysis shows that the amount of •OH radical decreased in the presence of N-CDs ( Fig. 2a), suggested that the •OH radicals react with N-CDs. The presence of N-CDs in Fenton reagent also resulted in lower 1O2 generation relative to Fenton system (Fig. 2b). The low amount of 1O2 indicates that •OH reacts with N-CDs and is not used to produce 1O2. In contrast, Fe3+ generate hydroperoxy radical (HO2 •) from reaction with H2O2 as given in reaction 2. In order to find the effect of HO2 • on CL-emission, Fe3+ was injected into a premixed solution of N-CDs and H2O2. A significantly low CL-emission was observed as compared to Fe2+ (Fig. 1aiv). This was due to the slow rate of reaction (2) than the rate of reaction (1) [24,30]. The formation of •OH from Fenton reaction is normally considered the most reactive intermediate. These data indicate that, the N-CDs coupled Fenton system can be effectively utilize for the detection of Fe2+ in solution.

The CL-emission intensity of N-CDs/H2O2 system were observed at different Fe2+ concentration ranging from 1.0 × 10−9–1.0 × 10−2 M ( Fig. 3a). The CL-intensity was increased within increase in Fe2+ concentration. A linear plot was obtained at two regions 1.0 × 10−9-1.0 × 10−6 M (Fig. 3b) and 1.0 × 10−5-1.0 × 10−2 M (Fig. S3). The two linear regions may be due to the difference in behavior of solute particles in solution at ultra-low and high concentration. Secondly, the dependency of CL emission on the concentration of reaction mixture at ultra-high and ultra-low level is different and is a possible reason for it. The limit of detection (LOD) is taken to be three time while limit of quantification (LOQ) was taken ten times the standard deviation in the blank solution, respectively [31,32]. The LOD was 0.2 × 10−9 M, (LOD = 3 s/k), while LOQ was 0.69 × 10−9 M, (LOQ = 10 s/k). Where “s” is standard deviation of blank and “k” is slope of calibration graph. As reported earlier, the LOD was assessed to be on the level of 6 × 10−6 M Fe2+ in aqueous samples [14], 0.8 × 10−9 M by on-line chelate resin pre-concentration method in sea water [33], 1.8 × 10−6 M by reversed flow spectrophotometric detection [3] and 8.9 × 10−7 M spectrophotometrically [2]. Relatively narrow linear analytical range constitutes limitations of the already developed methods. This method, have the advantage of expanded linear range, easy processing, and sensitivity. To evaluate the precision of the method, a set of 10 measurements of standard Fe2+ solution (1 ×10−7 M) was carried out. The relative standard deviation (RSD) was found to be 1.89% (Fig. S4). From the analytical point of view, the developed method allows the results to be obtained with high precision. These results show that N-CDs/H2O2 system have very promising application in the detection of Fe2+ ion in solution.

The selectivity of N-CDs/H2O2 system toward Fe2+ was analyzed with respect to Fe3+, Cr3+, Al3+ and divalent cations including Zn2+, Mg2+, Cu2+, Ca2+, Hg2+, Cd2+, Mn2+, Co2+, Pb2+, and Ni2+, Na+ and K+. Fig. 4a shows that, no significant CL-emission was observed for all tested cations. Moreover, the selectivity of the N-CDs/H2O2 system was also tested for Fe2+ in a mixture solution with other cations. A mixture solution consists of 1 × 10−3 M of Fe2+ and a tested cation was injected to N-CDs/H2O2. Fig. 4b shows the CL-emission intensity of the Fe2+ in the presence of interfering cations. A little high CL intensities in presence of Cu2+, Mn2+, Cr3+ and Co2+ were observed compared to only Fe2+ ion, whereas, no interference with other tested cations was observed. The high CL signals in presence of Cu, Mn, Cr, and Co may be due Fenton like reactions to produce •OH radicals and strong-redox behavior of these cations. However, yet compare to the CL-emission intensity of these interfering cations alone the CL-emission in combination with Fe2+ was significantly high, which indicate the selectivity of N-CDs/H2O2 system for detection of Fe2+ in solution. The capability of N-CDs/H2O2 was also tested for the interference from anions such as CO3 2−, HCO3 −, HSO3 −, NO2 −, I−, Cl−, NO3 −, SO4 2− and Br− at each concentration of 10−3 M. Fig. 4c shows no significant interference from the tested anions when used in a mixture solution with Fe2+. The high selectivity was attributed to ability of Fe2+ for •OH generation which was radially taken by N-CDs resulted in high CL-emission. The redox cycling of Fe2+ ion on the surface of N-CDs makes this system a useful sensing platform for selective detection of Fe2+ ion.

An estimation of Fe2+ in real water sample was done by injecting water sample to N-CDs/H2O2 solution. Fig. 5 show the CL-emission intensity obtained from the injection of water (100 µL) from various sources to N-CDs/H2O2 solution. Real samples often involve complex matrix that could interfere the accurate detection of the targeted analyte [34]. In order to evaluate the validity of the proposed method for the determination of Fe2+, recovery of Fe2+ in real samples was carried out. The effect of response of model calibration solutions with the response for matrix in studied water samples was checked by matrix effect [32]. Water samples was spiked with suitable addition of Fe2+ stock solution with different concentration so that the final value of Fe2+ concentration was established in range of 10−9–10−5 M Fe2+ ( Table 1). The fixed amount of Fe2+ was added to the real samples, and tried to find the recovery of Fe2+ in real samples. The recovery results were satisfactory. A little large standard error in real water samples may be due to the presence of oxidative species, which oxidizes Fe2+ ions, which were no more available for reaction to give CL signals. As reported by Pepper et al. [14] that the analytical procedure for Fe2+ determination must be oxygen free, since residual oxygen will easily oxidize trace-levels of Fe2+ and introduce significant error into the analysis. Thus, treatment of the real sample with a reducing agent prior to analysis will give accurate information of Fe2+ in solution.

A simple, fast, ecofriendly, sensing method for the detection of Fe2+ ion in solution was developed via CL using N-CDs/H2O2. This method detected Fe2+ with LOD of 0.2 × 10−9 M, LOQ of 0.69 × 10−9 M, and a dynamic linear range of 1 × 10−6 to 1 × 10−9 M. No significant interference was observed from most of the tested cations, and Fe3+ and also enabled the detection of Fe2+ in tap, lotus pond, and canal water samples. The non-laborious, use of eco-friendly reagents, cost-effective, selective, and sensitive detection make this method a significant tool for the analysis of Fe2+ in environmental samples. With continued refinement, the CL system could prove invaluable as an in situ monitoring tool to further explore temporal variations in Fe2+ speciation.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: This work was financially supported by National Natural Science Foundation of China (Nos. 21435002, 21621003) and Dr. S.N.A. Shah acknowledges China Postdoctoral Science Foundation.

ce:section.ce:para: The authors declare no competing financial interests.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data associated with this article can be found in the online version at doi:10.1016/j.talanta.2018.12.091.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Supplementary material The TEM Image (Fig. S1), UV–visible spectrum of the prepared N-CDs (Fig. S2), calibration curve of Fe2+ (Fig. S3) and the CL intensity of ten replicates of 1 × 10−7 M Fe2+ for RSD calculation (Fig. S4) are given in supporting information. These materials are available free of charge via the Internet at http://.
